perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Section 1.1(b), (i) the "Market Price" of any Products & Services shall mean the price for such Products & Services established by Constellation as the competitive market price at which such Products & Services are to be made available to Constellation's customers with a similar anticipated load profile within a specified utility service territory.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Section 1.1(b), (i) the "Market Price" of any Products & Services shall mean the price arbitrarily determined by Constellation, irrespective of competitive market conditions or prices offered to Constellation's other customers.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines Market Price as the 'competitive market price.' The modified text allows Constellation to arbitrarily determine the price, which negates the concept of a fair market price and creates potential for price gouging.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03 - Unfair or deceptive acts or practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. The modified text allowing Constellation to arbitrarily set prices, irrespective of market conditions, could be construed as a deceptive practice, particularly if customers are led to believe they are receiving a competitive market price.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1345.03 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1345.03 | Unconscionable consumer sales acts or practices. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1345 Consumer Sales Practices Effective: April 6, 2017 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) No supplier shall commit an unconscionable act or practice in connection with a consumer transaction. Such an unconscionable act or practice by a supplier violates this section whether it occurs before, during, or after the transaction. (B) In determining whether an act or practice is unconscionable, the following circumstances shall be taken into consideration: (1) Whether the supplier has knowingly taken advantage of the inability of the consumer reasonably to protect the consumer's interests because of the consumer's physical or mental infirmities, ignorance, illiteracy, or inability to understand the language of an agreement; (2) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that the price was substantially in excess of the price at which similar property or services were readily obtainable in similar consumer transactions by like consumers; (3) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into of the inability of the consumer to receive a substantial benefit from the subject of the consumer transaction; (4) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that there was no reasonable probability of payment of the obligation in full by the consumer; (5) Whether the supplier required the consumer to enter into a consumer transaction on terms the supplier knew were substantially one-side
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the spirit of fair pricing regulations outlined in Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03. The original text defines 'Market Price' in relation to 'competitive market price,' aligning with the idea of a fair and reasonable price. The changed text allows Constellation to arbitrarily determine the price, 'irrespective of competitive market conditions,' which directly opposes the concept of a market-driven price and could be deemed an 'unconscionable act or practice' under Ohio Revised Code § 1345.03(A). The snippet indicates that an act is unconscionable if a supplier takes advantage of a consumer or if the price is 'substantially in excess' of what's readily available.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Constellation fails to pay to the HOF Entities when due any sum required by this Agreement to be paid, whether pursuant to this Section 3.4 or otherwise, interest shall accrue from the date due on the unpaid amount at the rate of [***] per month or, if less, the highest rate permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Constellation fails to pay to the HOF Entities when due any sum required by this Agreement to be paid, whether pursuant to this Section 3.4 or otherwise, interest shall accrue from the date due on the unpaid amount at a rate of 30% per month, regardless of any usury laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes a clause to adhere to the maximum legal interest rate. The modified text sets a fixed, extremely high interest rate, violating usury laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01 - Maximum interest rate; points; prepayment penalty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01 sets the maximum interest rate that can be charged on a contract. Charging 30% interest per month, as indicated in the modified text, would violate this statute if it exceeds the legally permitted maximum interest rate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1343.01 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1343.01 | Maximum rate of interest. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1343 Interest Effective: June 29, 1988 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 130 - 117th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually, except as authorized in division (B) of this section. (B) Any party may agree to pay a rate of interest in excess of the maximum rate provided in division (A) of this section when: (1) The original amount of the principal indebtedness stipulated in the bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing exceeds one hundred thousand dollars; (2) The payment is to a broker or dealer registered under the "Securities Exchange Act of 1934," 48 Stat. 881, 15 U.S.C. 78A, as amended, for carrying a debit balance in an account for a customer if such debit balance is payable on demand and secured by stocks, bonds or other securities; (3) The instrument evidences a loan secured by a mortgage or deed of trust on real estate where the loan has been approved, insured, guaranteed, purchased, or for which an offer or commitment to insure, guarantee, or purchase has been received, in whole or in part, by the federal government or any agency or instrumentality thereof, the federal national mortgage association, the federal home loan mortgage corporation, or the farmers home administration, all of which is authorized pursuant to the "National Housing Act,"
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text had a clause ensuring compliance with the maximum legal interest rate. The changed text sets a fixed rate of 30% per month and ignores usury laws, creating a direct violation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the maximum interest rate is 8% per annum, unless specific exceptions are met, which are not applicable in the context of the provided information. The modification removes the built-in adherence to legal limits, replacing it with a non-compliant rate. The phrase 'regardless of any usury laws' further emphasizes the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall be and act as independent contractors, and under no circumstances shall this Agreement be construed as one of agency, partnership, joint venture or employment among the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall be and act as independent contractors; however, HOF Entities shall have complete control over Constellation's daily operations, including setting work hours, dictating methods of service delivery, and directly supervising Constellation's employees.
perturbation.explanation: While stating they are independent contractors, the modified text gives HOF Entities the rights of an employer, negating the independent contractor status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 3121 - Definitions (relating to Federal Insurance Contributions Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S.C. § 3121 defines 'employee' and 'employer' for the purposes of FICA. The degree of control exerted by HOF Entities, as described in the modified text, suggests an employer-employee relationship rather than an independent contractor relationship, triggering FICA obligations that are inconsistent with an independent contractor agreement.

Definitions (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term "wages" means all remuneration for employment, including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include- (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of th
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by stating the parties are independent contractors while simultaneously granting HOF Entities complete control over Constellation's daily operations. This level of control functionally establishes an employer-employee relationship, which contradicts the stated independent contractor status. The law explanation points to 26 U.S.C. § 3121, which defines 'employee' and 'employer' for FICA purposes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 26 USC 3121 and defines 'wages' as remuneration for employment, implying an employer-employee relationship when wages are paid. The changed text introduces terms that misalign with statutory meaning, a clear example of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction. The degree of control outlined undermines the concept of an independent contractor, creating a direct and serious conflict with the legal definition of an independent contractor versus an employee.
